nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs; perches on heads of small branching corals such as Stylophora, Pocillopora and Acropora.

decorPreferences: open swimming space above the rockwork

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide variety of meaty foods including frozen mysis, krill, chopped seafood and quality marine carnivore pellets. Live feeder shrimp may be offered occasionally.

goodWith: wrasses of similar or larger size
avoidWith: very small fish that can fit in its mouth
other hawkfish in the same tank

imageCandidates.notes: Nick Hobgood (Nhobgood), 3714x2611. Tack-sharp, vibrant orange-red individual perched in branching Acropora; clearly shows the diagnostic orange/blue/red eye-arc, white lateral stripe and orange gill dashes. Best overall.

Henrik Kibak, 2048x1495. Sharp full-body lateral view including the purple-tinted caudal fin, reddish-brown morph on branching coral; arc-eye mark and white stripe clearly visible. Different angle/individual.
Rickard Zerpe, 1570x1178. Crisp whole-fish shot of a paler yellowish variant showing the bright yellow-and-blue eye-arc and white stripe; adds colour-morph variety.

summary: Compact reef-perching predator from the Indo-Pacific with a distinctive orange-and-blue eye arc; hardy and easy to keep but will hunt small ornamental shrimp and crabs.
careNotes: Generally considered an easy, hardy fish suited to fish-only and reef tanks that do not house small ornamental invertebrates. Spends most of its time perched on live rock or coral branches rather than swimming in open water, so aquascaping with multiple perching points is more important than tank depth. It is generally advised to introduce the Arc Eye last to an established community, as it tends to be territorial and has been known to harass new or smaller tankmates. Reef compatibility is the main caveat: hawkfish are dedicated ambush hunters of small crustaceans and will pick off ornamental shrimp, small hermit crabs, and tiny crabs in most reef setups. Corals and other sessile (attached, non-moving) invertebrates are typically left alone. A tightly fitting lid is worth considering, since perching fish occasionally launch themselves toward prey.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. In the wild, pairs perform a brief spawning ascent of about 0.4–1.0 m into the water column to release pelagic (free-floating) eggs. No established captive-rearing protocol has been widely reported.

additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size guidance; this source recommends ~180 L (~50 gal) minimum vs LiveAquaria's 30 gal floor, motivating the 200 L final figure.

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive breeding protocol; pelagic spawner with brief paired spawning ascent in the wild.
